epigraph.text: Two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their toil.

content.text:
Just as certain pairs of numbers have special relationships (like 3 and 7 
summing to 10), certain pairs of angles have special relationships defined 
by how their measures combine. These relationships appear throughout 
geometry and will be essential for proving theorems.

content.title: Complementary Angles
content.content:
Two angles are **complementary** if the 
sum of their measures is $90°$. Each angle is called the **complement** 
of the other.

content.title: Supplementary Angles
content.content:
Two angles are **supplementary** if the 
sum of their measures is $180°$. Each angle is called the **supplement** 
of the other.

content.text:
**Memory tip:** "C" comes before "S" in the alphabet, and 90 
comes before 180 on the number line. Complementary = 90°, Supplementary = 180°.

content.title: Finding complements and supplements
content.problem: Find the complement and supplement of a $37°$ angle.

content.title: Using complementary angles
content.problem:
Two angles are complementary. One angle measures $3x + 5$ degrees and the 
other measures $2x - 10$ degrees. Find the measure of each angle.
content.solution:
Since the angles are complementary:
\[
 (3x + 5) + (2x - 10) = 90
\]
\[
 5x - 5 = 90
\]
\[
 x = 19
\]

The angles measure $3(19) + 5 = 62°$ and $2(19) - 10 = 28°$.

Check: $62° + 28° = 90°$

content.text:
When two lines intersect, they form two pairs of **vertical angles**%
.

content.title: Vertical Angles
content.content:
**Vertical angles** are two nonadjacent angles formed by two 
intersecting lines.

content.text:
In the figure, $\angle 1$ and $\angle 3$ are vertical angles, as are 
$\angle 2$ and $\angle 4$.

content.content:
Consider two intersecting lines forming angles $1$, $2$, $3$, $4$ as shown above.

$\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ are supplementary (they form a straight angle), so:
\[
 m\angle 1 + m\angle 2 = 180°
\]

$\angle 2$ and $\angle 3$ are also supplementary, so:
\[
 m\angle 2 + m\angle 3 = 180°
\]

From these equations:
\[
 m\angle 1 + m\angle 2 = m\angle 2 + m\angle 3
\]

Subtracting $m\angle 2$ from both sides:
\[
 m\angle 1 = m\angle 3
\]

Therefore, $\angle 1 \cong \angle 3$. Similarly, $\angle 2 \cong \angle 4$.

content.title: Using vertical angles
content.problem:
Two lines intersect. One of the angles measures $67°$. Find the measures 
of the other three angles.
content.solution:
Let the given angle be $\angle 1$ with $m\angle 1 = 67°$.

The angle vertical to it: $m\angle 3 = 67°$ (vertical angles are congruent)

The angles adjacent to it are supplementary:
\[
 m\angle 2 = 180° - 67° = 113°
\]
\[
 m\angle 4 = 180° - 67° = 113°
\]

The four angles measure $67°$, $113°$, $67°$, $113°$.

content.title: Linear Pair
content.content:
A **linear pair** consists of two adjacent angles 
whose noncommon sides are opposite rays.

content.title: Linear Pair Postulate
content.content: If two angles form a linear pair, they are supplementary.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This postulate is almost immediate from the definitions: the noncommon 
sides form a straight angle of $180°$.

content.title: Adjacent Angles
content.content:
**Adjacent angles** are two angles that:

1. Share a common vertex

2. Share a common side

3. Have no interior points in common

content.text:
Not all adjacent angles are supplementary—only those forming a linear 
pair are guaranteed to be supplementary.

marginNotes.text:
It is good to grasp the one and not 
let go of the other.
